videos.title: I Birthed a new Agent Profile with the Hermes Desktop App
videos.description:
I set up my first dedicated content creation agent in Hermes: Alex.

The idea is simple: take my streams and have an agent help repurpose them into clips, blogs, X posts, Reddit posts, and scheduled content. I create a new Hermes Profile, leave the soul.md blank, then use interview mode so the agent can ask me questions and help define its own operating instructions.

In this setup, Alex gets a clear role: grow TechFren, be technically correct, stay truth-seeking, and produce consistent content. Then I start wiring up the practical access it needs: X.com, Reddit, remote screen share, and Playwright MCP through Chrome.

videos.title: Hermes Kanban Actually Started Running My Content Work
videos.description:
Hermes Kanban turned my stream backlog into real content tasks, dispatched worker processes, and left logs/handoffs I could inspect.

In this cut, I stress-test Hermes built-in Kanban as a content-ops pipeline for Alex, my 24/7 Hermes content agent. Streams become cards, the dispatcher starts moving work, worker logs show where the work went, and the biggest decision becomes operational: content should stay owned by Alex instead of being split across random specialist profiles.

The useful takeaway is the operating model: use Kanban for durable task state, logs, and handoffs, while keeping creative judgment and final ownership in the profile that actually understands the channel.

TechFren: https://techfren.net
Hermes Agent docs: https://hermes-agent.nousresearch.com/docs

videos.title: My Hermes Agent was BLOATED
videos.description:
I audited 118 skills in my Hermes/Alex content-agent profile, removed the bloat, and verified the profile was down to 69 skills.

This is the practical cleanup pass: open the Hermes dashboard, review what the Alex profile can actually use, build a delete list, keep creator-specific tools, remove stale default and dev-only skills, then hand the deletion back to Hermes with safer reasoning enabled.

The rule I use here is simple: every skill has to earn its place in the profile it serves. A skill can be useful in general and still be wrong for Alex.

The cut ends with the before/after proof, plus a short look at Hermes Skill Curator as the maintenance layer for keeping agent-created skills from piling up again.

Original steam : https://youtube.com/live/mQvAsmZHiO0?feature=share
TechFren: https://techfren.net
Hermes Agent docs: https://hermes-agent.nousresearch.com/docs

videos.title: Setting Up Tailscale for My Remote Agent
videos.description:
I tried running Hermes Desktop as a proactive 24/7 AI agent on my MacBook Air while I was traveling.

The setup worked at first, but I made one big mistake: the only way I had to talk to the agent remotely was through Discord. A day or two into the trip, that channel broke, and I had no way to connect back to the machine running the agent.

This is why I’m setting up Tailscale as a secure remote access layer for my local AI agent setup.

If you’re experimenting with always-on agents, local AI tools, self-hosted workflows, or desktop agents like Hermes, remote access and fallback infrastructure matter way more than you think.

videos.title: Hermes Desktop Actually Works Better Than I Expected
videos.description:
I spent over eight months building my own desktop agent workflow. Then I tried Hermes Desktop live, and within the first session I was seriously considering switching.

This is my first real test of Hermes Agent Desktop from Nous Research. I compare it against the agent workflow I’ve been building, test the desktop UI, voice conversation, text-to-speech, speech-to-text, memory/context settings, skills, profiles, and the ability to create a new AJ profile with my existing agent setup.

The wild part: Hermes didn’t just feel polished. It hit the exact layer I care about most, which is persistent agent context, profiles, skills, and long-running workflows that can actually help move goals forward.

It was not flawless. I hit a couple bugs, including missing skills and some trust-friction around credentials. But for a live first try, this was much stronger than I expected.

Original Stream: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lEOTCZgxdho
Hermes Agent: https://hermes-agent.nousresearch.com/docs/

I Ran The Olympics For AI Coding
videos.description:
I'm building the Olympics for AI coding. Last weekend, the first event (Vibe Code Cup) challenged competitors to build a chess engine under 1MB in just 2 hours using AI tools. The winning submission took a genius shortcut: finding tomitankChess on GitHub (a 2900+ ELO JavaScript engine by Tamás Kuzmics, built over 9 years) and adapting it in a single prompt. This video breaks down why the edge in AI coding isn't the tools - it's the human judgment behind them.

videos.title: Smallest.ai hackathon submission
FULLY free clawdbot (Host & API Tokens) in under 5 minutes
videos.description:
Clawdbot is officially gone—meet Moltbot. After Anthropic started restricting Max plan access and trademark issues arose, I had to pivot fast. In this video, I introduce a new "hack" using Moonshot AI’s Kimi 2.5 model. It is currently free to try for 7 days and allegedly beats Claude 3 Opus in benchmarks.

I’ll walk you through how to generate a Kimi API key, configure it locally with your Discord bot, and fix the JSON settings using the config link below. We also test the bot's ability to create memes and videos directly in Discord. Finally, I cover important updates regarding the AWS Free Tier time limits, how to triple your AWS storage volume for free, and why I’m planning a migration to Oracle Cloud’s "Always Free" tier for better RAM.

Important Links:
Start Kimi Trial: https://www.kimi.com/
Get Kimi API Key: https://www.kimi.com/code
Moltbot Config JSON: https://pastebin.com/raw/uXcCHhZQ

videos.title: Clawdbot x Meta Rayban x Amazon | From Photo to Purchase
videos.description:
first video/tutorial : https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=3DBpfB0ao50
I finally successfully automated Amazon ordering using my Meta Ray-Ban glasses! In this video, I demonstrate a custom workflow where I take a photo of a product (peppermint gum) via WhatsApp, send it to my AI agent "Jinx," and have it automatically add the item to my Amazon cart.

Timestamps
00:00:00 - Demo: Scanning gum with glasses
00:00:50 - AI agent identifies the product
00:01:03 - Activating ClawdBot Chrome Extension
00:01:21 - Gum added to Amazon cart
00:01:50 - Local Mac vs AWS VPS
00:02:30 - No SDK needed: WhatsApp method
00:02:44 - Tutorial: ClawdBot Extension setup
00:03:20 - NPM install and loading extension
00:04:10 - Remote gateway via Cloudflare Tunnels
00:04:50 - Security risks and sandboxing
00:05:40 - Hardware costs: Mac Mini vs VPS

To bypass Amazon’s aggressive bot detection, I used the ClawdBot Chrome extension to control an authenticated browser session. I’ll walk you through the technical setup, including installing the extension via NPM, connecting my local machine to an AWS VPS using Cloudflare Tunnels (or Ngrok), and the trade-offs between running a Mac Mini 24/7 versus a cloud server.
Keywords: Meta Ray-Ban Smart Glasses, Amazon Automation, ClawdBot, WhatsApp Bot, Web Scraping, AWS VPS, Cloudflare Tunnels, Chrome Extension, AI Agent, Jinx.

videos.title: riverside tech magic episode   jan 08, 2026 arash's studio
Why I Think Skills Will Replace 80% of MCPs
videos.description:
In Part 3 of this research series, I double down on my controversial conclusion: Anthropic Skills can replace 80% of Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers. Despite skepticism from the community, my latest benchmarks prove that replacing MCPs with Skills results in massive context savings without sacrificing performance.

I put this theory to the test by running a "Wordle Bench" comparing the Playwright MCP against Sawyer’s Dev Browser Skill. We also live-code a custom Exa API skill to see if we can replicate MCP functionality in under 10 minutes. The results show a 10x reduction in token usage and faster execution times, challenging the current consensus on how AI agents should interact with tools.

videos.title: Claude/Agent Skills Cut My Context Window by 98%
videos.description:
I benchmarked Playwright MCP, Claude Skills, the Claude Chrome Extension, and CLI alternatives on my Wordle Bench to find out which is fastest, cheapest, and least bloated.

Skills use progressive loading (90 tokens vs 7,000), but MCPs have no cold start. There are real trade-offs either way — and Anthropic says both still matter.

⏱️ TIMESTAMPS:

🎯 KEY TAKEAWAYS
→ Skills = less context bloat (90 tokens vs 7,000)
→ MCPs = no cold start, server runs immediately
→ Chrome Extension = expensive but handles screenshots
→ CLI/Bash = minimal overhead, maximum control
→ MCPs are becoming APIs for agents, Skills teach what to do with them

🔗 LINKS & RESOURCES
Sawyer Hood's Dev Browser Skill: https://github.com/SawyerHood/dev-browser

Claude Chrome Extension: https://chromewebstore.google.com/detail/claude

videos.title: I Finally Shipped SpeakMCP v1 — Here's Everything It Does
videos.description:
I am incredibly excited to finally launch SpeakMCP v1! After six months of development, this fully open-source native desktop app is ready to change how you interact with AI. Think of it as a bridge between voice control and the Model Context Protocol (MCP).
SpeakMCP allows you to spawn AI agents to control your terminal, manage GitHub issues, dictate notes into Notion, and interact with any third-party service—all with your voice or keyboard. It runs on Windows, Mac, and Linux, and supports local models like LlamaCPP or cloud providers like Groq and OpenAI. Whether you want to translate speech in real-time or run 5 coding agents in parallel, this tool puts completely general agents at your fingertips.

Download SpeakMCP: https://github.com/aj47/SpeakMCP/releases
Join the Discord: https://discord.gg/cK9WeQ7jPq

videos.title: I discovered a new benchmark...
videos.description:
I discovered a fun benchmark for agentic AI and LLMs: Can they solve the New York Times Wordle? In this stream highlight, I put Grok-4 Fast to the test using my open-source MCP client, Speak MCP, connected to a Playwright server via Open Router.

Watch as the AI agent navigates the web, plays the game, and makes a clutch last-minute guess to solve the puzzle. This is the start of the 'Wordlebench', where I'll test different models to see who is the smartest (and fastest) Wordle solver. What AI should I try next?

videos.title: Playwright MCP vs. Chrome Dev Tools: Which is Better for Finding Bugs?
videos.description:
Timestamps:
00:00 - Intro
00:08 - Comparing Playwright to Chrome DevTools
01:06 - Giving the agent its prompt
01:45 - Running the app with Playwright
02:50 - Playwright using my own Chrome session
03:22 - Reviewing Playwright's findings

03:59 - Playwright vs. Chrome DevTools tools
05:35 - Chrome DevTools' initial actions

06:30 - Why I prefer Playwright
07:09 - Where Chrome DevTools is better
07:25 - My final conclusion

References:
Link to Playwright: https://github.com/microsoft/playwright-mcp
Link to Chrome DevTools: https://github.com/ChromeDevTools/chrome-devtools-mcp

In this video, we put two powerful browser automation tools, Playwright and Chrome DevTools, to the test to see which one is better for finding bugs in a web application. We'll give both tools the same prompt and compare their processes and results.
We'll explore the key differences between Playwright and Chrome DevTools, including their unique features and toolsets. Discover which tool offers more granular control for debugging and which one might be better for your specific use case. If you're deciding between these two popular automation frameworks, this video will give you the insights you need.
